data.title: Reintroducing An Old Divide: State-Owned Vs. Market-Owned Capitalism
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

For most U.S. investors, the concept of state ownership feels foreign, almost anachronistic. America's economic mythology rests on the idea that markets allocate capital more efficiently than governments ever could. The implicit hierarchy is clear: privately owned equals dynamic and efficient; state-owned equals bureaucratic and stagnant. But step outside the U.S., and this binary quickly blurs. Across much of the world, especially in emerging markets, the state is not merely a regulator of markets but a participant, shareholder and sometimes the commanding force behind entire sectors.

The Modern State as Capital Allocator

In economies like China's, the state remains deeply embedded in the corporate landscape. State-owned enterprises (SOEs)1 dominate sectors such as energy, banking, telecommunications and infrastructure. These companies are not just profit-seeking entities; they are instruments of national policy, used to stabilize employment, secure resources and project geopolitical influence. By contrast, non-state-owned enterprises (NSOEs), often more entrepreneurial and export-oriented, compete for capital and innovation on market terms. Understanding the balance between these two corporate archetypes is increasingly vital, especially as global investors reassess where true economic dynamism resides.

Why the Distinction Matters Now

The distinction between state and non-state capitalism has become a quiet but powerful driver of returns in emerging markets. Over the past decade, NSOEs have tended to deliver stronger growth, higher profitability and better capital discipline, traits that resonate with Western investors. Yet SOEs, backed by policy support and protected market share, can dominate in periods of macro volatility or strategic realignment. As the global economy fractures into regional blocs and the state reasserts itself even in advanced economies, from industrial policy to energy transition, the old boundary between public and private capital may be less absolute than it once seemed.

The Principal-Agent Problem

Figure 1 captures the essence of the principal–agent problem as it applies to state-owned enterprises. In theory, the government, as the principal, owns the enterprise on behalf of its citizens, delegating control to professional managers and boards who act as agents. In practice, however, the agents' incentives often diverge from pure profit maximization. Governments may direct firms to prioritize macroeconomic or social goals, such as sustaining employment, supporting gross domestic product (GDP) targets or preventing bankruptcies, even when these conflict with shareholder value. This introduces a structural tension at the heart of SOEs: are they run for the public good, as defined by policymakers, or for shareholders, who expect returns on capital? The Economist's framing distills the paradox perfectly: state ownership transforms a simple agency problem into a multi-layered struggle between politics, economics and accountability.2

Figure 1: Introducing the Principal-Agent Problem

From Theory to Practice: The Principal–Agent Problem in Action

The principal–agent problem, the misalignment between owners' goals and managers' incentives, often manifests most clearly in state-owned enterprises. PetroChina (PCCYF) and Exxon Mobil (XOM) offer a clean, real-world contrast between a state-owned and a shareholder-driven oil major.3

Scale vs. Efficiency

Figure 2 tells a striking story of scale and efficiency divergence.

  • PetroChina employs roughly 370,800 people, compared to Exxon Mobil's 61,000, a more than sixfold difference.
  • Yet, this workforce scale doesn't translate into proportionally higher output or profitability.

State ownership often prioritizes social goals like employment stability and national development over operational efficiency, whereas Exxon Mobil's leaner structure reflects a private-sector focus on shareholder value and capital productivity.

Figure 2: PetroChina Employs Six Times More People than Exxon Mobil

Productivity: A Study in Incentives

Figure 3 highlights revenue per employee, a strong proxy for productivity and managerial discipline.

  • Exxon Mobil generates about $5.7 million per employee, versus $1.1 million at PetroChina. That's a fivefold efficiency gap, signaling the influence of incentives: Exxon's compensation and culture are tied tightly to returns and innovation; PetroChina's management, by contrast, balances state employment mandates, energy security goals and geopolitical considerations—objectives often orthogonal to profit maximization.

Figure 3: Exxon Mobil Generated Five Times More Revenue per Worker than PetroChina

Profitability Metrics: Incentive Alignment in the Numbers

The profitability chart cements the pattern:

  • Exxon Mobil posts a 9.6% net margin and 14.4% return on equity (ROE).
  • PetroChina lags at 5.6% net margin and 11.1% ROE.

These differences may look modest, but they compound dramatically over time. Exxon's shareholder accountability drives capital discipline and cost control, while PetroChina's governance, anchored in state ownership, tends to blur accountability and dilute incentives to optimize capital efficiency.

Figure 4: Profitability Gaps between Market-Driven and State-Directed Models

In 2025, state-owned enterprises remain concentrated in a few large markets and policy-sensitive sectors. As shown in figure 5, China, Saudi Arabia and India account for the bulk of state-linked equity capitalization, underscoring how national policy priorities shape corporate scale in emerging markets. On the right, the sectoral mix reveals a heavy tilt toward energy, financials and industrials, areas where governments view strategic control as essential. Together, these distributions highlight that the state's corporate footprint remains focused on power, capital and infrastructure, the commanding heights of the emerging-market economy.

Figure 5: Where the State Still Dominates: Geography and Sector Concentration of Emerging-Market SOEs (2025)

Ownership Structure as Investment Edge: The Case for ex-SOE Exposure

The WisdomTree Emerging Markets ex-State-Owned Enterprises Fund (XSOE)4 was built around a simple but powerful premise: ownership matters. In many emerging markets, governments retain material stakes, often more than 20%, in large, listed companies, shaping priorities toward political or strategic goals such as employment, subsidies or national control rather than shareholder value.

The result is not simply a cleaner index; it's a structural refinement of EM beta: the same countries but with stronger governance, better capital efficiency and fewer policy distortions. Over time, this focus on incentive alignment aims to improve return on equity and margin sustainability, giving investors a purer expression of capitalism in markets where state involvement often dilutes it.

Repricing Governance: Why XSOE Trades with Stronger Fundamentals than EM

When investors think about equity benchmarks, the S&P 500 Index, with its exposure to U.S. equities, tends to define the high-quality, growth-oriented standard. Yet, what's striking in the data is how XSOE narrows the traditional gap between developed and emerging markets. In figure 6, we see XSOE trades at a discount to the S&P 500, 17.8x earnings vs. 23.8x, and 11.5x cash flow vs. 19.5x, but modestly richer than the broad MSCI Emerging Markets Index benchmark. That premium is rational: by excluding state-owned firms, XSOE skews toward more capital-efficient, shareholder-aligned businesses, the same qualities investors pay up for in developed markets.

Figure 6: How XSOE Sits between EM and the U.S. Equity Markets on Valuation

The payoff shows up in figure 7, where we see the quality and growth metrics. Return on equity sits at 13.7%, above the 12.5% of MSCI EM and closer to the S&P's 18.3%, while long-term earnings growth expectations reach 13.3%, well above both comparators. These companies are smaller on average but far more disciplined in capital use, generating higher profitability from each dollar of equity and reinvesting at stronger rates of return.

Figure 7: Governance as a Driver of Growth

Conclusion: The Future of EM Investing Belongs to Entrepreneurs, Not Bureaucrats

In the end, the ex-state-owned approach is about more than just filtering a dataset; it's about redefining what it means to invest in emerging markets. Traditional EM exposure often ties investors to the ambitions of governments; XSOE ties them to the ambitions of entrepreneurs. It channels capital toward companies competing on innovation, efficiency and global relevance rather than policy mandate or political favor. This is ideally where growth compounds cleanly, where governance, profitability and market incentives align. In a world where emerging markets are no longer just catch-up stories but laboratories of technological and business model evolution, the ex-SOE framework separates the engines of progress from the machinery of policy.

Footnotes

1 In this article, we define state-owned enterprises as those companies with greater than 20% ownership by a state-entity.

2 Source: "Government-Controlled Firms: State Capitalism in the Dock," The Economist, 11/20/14.

3 Data points regarding Exxon Mobil and PetroChina are sourced from Exxon Mobil Corporation, "2024 Annual Report" (Form 10-K), 4/7/25;PetroChina Company Limited,"2024 Annual Report," 4/25. Subject to change.

4 XSOE is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree Emerging Markets ex-State-Owned Enterprises Index.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Investments in emerging or offshore markets are generally less liquid and less efficient than investments in developed markets and are subject to additional risks, such as risks of adverse governmental regulation and intervention or political developments. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors and/or regions increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. Investments in currency involve additional special risks, such as credit risk and interest rate fluctuations. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit, and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Inches Up For Second Straight Month
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment inched up for a second straight month in January but remains nearly 25% below where it was a year ago. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index rose 1.1 points (2.1%) to 54.0 this month. The latest reading was higher than the forecast of 53.5.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regard to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment inched up for the second straight month and reached its highest reading since September 2025. Improvements in January were seen among lower-income consumers, while sentiment fell for those with higher incomes. All told, while consumers perceived some modest improvement in the economy over the past two months, their sentiment remains nearly 25% below last January’s reading. They continue to be focused primarily on kitchen table issues, like high prices and softening labor markets. Although consumers’ worries about tariffs appear to be gradually receding, they remain guarded about the overall strength of business conditions and labor markets. Note that more than 90% of interviews for this release were collected prior to the capture of Maduro in Venezuela.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. The current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 35.8% below its average reading of 84.1 (arithmetic mean) and 34.9% below its geometric mean of 82.9, based on data dating back to 1978.

To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In January, the CECI rose for the first time in six months to 52.4. This represents a 4.0% decrease from the previous month but a -29.2% decline from a year ago.

Meanwhile, the CEI rose for a third straight month to 55.0. This represents a 0.7% increase from the previous month but a -20.5% drop from one year ago.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations held steady in January at 4.2%. This is the lowest reading since January 2025 but remains well above that month’s 3.3%. Long-run inflation expectations ticked up slightly from 3.2% in December to 3.4% this month. In comparison, readings ranged between 2.8% and 3.2% in 2024, and were below 2.8% throughout 2019 and 2020.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB Business Optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include: Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cabot: Maintaining Bearish Stance On Guidance Cut And Enduring Trade Turbulence
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote about Cabot Corporation (CBT) in October of last year, before the release of the company's fourth-quarter earnings report (11/3/2025). Bearish technical trends, volume pressure in both the Reinforcement Materials & Performance Chemicals segments, and tariff uncertainty were principal reasons why we downgraded the specialty chemicals company to a 'Sell'. Shares are down roughly 3.1% since our bearish call, and we fear more downside may be looming due to the following reasons.

Firstly, the company's earnings trajectory. Operating profit came in down in both Reinforcement Materials & Performance Chemicals in the fourth quarter due to below-average volumes in both segments. Sluggish end-market demand is expected to continue in 2026, with management announcing guidance now coming in at a much lower $6 to $7 in earnings per share, as opposed to the much higher $7.15 to $7.50 range given on the Q3 earnings call. Although quite the drop, management must be applauded here for recognising prolonged adverse trading conditions and being straight with its investor base. Furthermore, the stated adjusted EPS of $7.25 was an impressive number for Cabot in fiscal 2025, considering, as mentioned, how volumes were down compared to the previous year.

Global Turbulence Continues

Market conditions aside, management continues in its endeavors to control the controllables. The decision to acquire Bridgestone's reinforcing carbon plant in Mexico is expected to meaningfully aid Cabot's financials in time. Cabot's Battery Materials business continues to take advantage of industry tailwinds, with the lithium battery space expected to grow at an elevated clip over the next three years.

However, global trade turbulence is expected to continue going forward, as we see from current trends governing contract negotiations, pricing clarity, as well as margin headwinds from tire imports (consequently reducing domestic production in the West). Again, the CEO must be applauded here for not pointing to any near-term turnaround, as we see below

Automotive production in the Western economies contracted in 2025, and elevated Asian tire imports into Western geographies continue to persist. Additionally, global manufacturing PMI was in or near contraction territory for most of 2025, and the expected interest rate cut cycle was slower than expected, leaving the housing and construction sector in a trough. In addition, 2025 was characterised by global trade turbulence, which is making it very difficult to determine long-term, durable demand levels. As we look to 2026, we don't yet see signs of improvement across these dimensions.

Concerning Long-Term Technicals

Cabot's long-term technicals echo the above comments, as we see from the 20-year chart. Not long ago, Cabot stock registered a long-term death cross (crossing of the 10-month moving average beneath its 40-week counterpart) after a bearish MACD crossover early last year. These two events confirm that a trend change is definitely in motion, where the $55 level approx. now looks like a downside target unless higher highs can swiftly prevail.

Poor Near-Term Growth Expectations

Therefore, what investors have to size up here is the total return potential of the stock as opposed to only taking note of Cabot's cheap valuation and covered dividend. An excellent way to do this is to use the following formula, where CBT's

'Expected Rate Of Return' = G + D/M

where G = Cabot's expected earnings growth rate next year, D equates to the company's GAAP dividend payout ratio, and M denotes the GAAP earnings multiple. Since management has already guided negative bottom-line growth for fiscal 2026, we can state that Cabot's expected rate of return

= 0 + 29/11.64 = 2.49%

Two things the above estimation tells us. Firstly, the expected rate of return comes in well below a fixed-income alternative (like the 10-year US Treasury Bond), which is risk-free, all things remaining equal. Secondly, Cabot's low dividend payout ratio demonstrates that the company's return on incremental capital may, in fact, be much lower than what investors think. What we mean here is that sometimes we cut some slack to no-growth companies when they are reporting high payout ratios. We do this because of the inherent potential the company may have in increasing returns from elevated investment. In Cabot's situation, however, we believe the company's low payout ratio offers reduced scope to invest its way out of trouble swiftly.

Long-Term Hold Doesn't Look Viable

Now, we acknowledge that some long-term investors may desire to ride out the near-term volatility while continuing to reinvest the 2.57% yield on offer over multiple years. Although CBT's dividend growth rates may have subsided in recent times, one can't dismiss that the company has paid a dividend for 36 consecutive years, with growth materialising in 14 of those years.

Now, let's say our long-term investor wants to earn 10% annually over the next decade, which means Cabot stock would have to be trading at approximately $182 per share in January 2036. Furthermore, let's say our investor would sell the investment for a maximum of 12 times its GAAP earnings at the time (which is right in line with Cabot's average earnings multiple over the past 5 years). Dividing $182 by 12 gives us annual earnings of $15.17 per share 10 years from now. Bridging the gap between the expected $6.50 in earnings next year and the $15.17 per share 10 years from now means CBT would have to deliver an annual growth rate of 8.84% going forward.

Suffice it to say, although CBT may look attractive on the surface from a valuation standpoint, look at the earnings growth CBT would have to deliver for our long-term investor to book a 10% average annual return. Furthermore, although we would not discount the possibility of the above happening (if fundamentals were to drastically change), near-term trends and concerning technicals point to muted buying volume for now, all things remaining equal.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, we are reiterating our bearish stance in CBT due to negative earnings growth, bearish technicals, and sustained volume pressure in both segments in recent months. Furthermore, investors may be underrating the excellent cost-cutting initiatives the company has undergone, which have protected core profitability as we stand. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Constellation Brands: Still Trading Well Below Fair Value Despite Solid Execution
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Constellation Brands (STZ), I reiterated their Buy rating thanks to their strong fundamentals, leadership backed by premium brands, and long-term upside potential as a result of macro improvements.

With a better-than-expected earnings report released recently that beat the market’s expectations, the company remains in an even better position to take advantage of long-term tailwinds despite the near-term macro uncertainty and pressure, which is why I’ll reiterate STZ’s Buy rating.

Internal Developments

STZ’s Q3’FY26 report came with a double-beat, topping the market’s revenue and EPS estimates, although the revenue is still down ~9.8% YoY as a result of macro factors that I’ll mention below, with drops across all segments of the business.

The company lowered their EPS guidance quite significantly to $11.30 to $11.60 for FY26, while continuing to expect about $1.35 billion in free cash flow at midpoint, although that comes at a time with higher CAPEX and operating issues caused by tariffs and a weak consumer environment. This would place them at a P/FCF ratio of 18.1, which is not terrible given their higher potential once the environment recovers.

The company continued their significant dividends and buybacks, returning ~$1.36 billion to its shareholders during the 9M’FY26 period, although that’s already pretty much everything they expect to make during this fiscal year, while still having about $500 million in estimated CAPEX left.

Financially, based on STZ’s latest report, we see some improvements, with the current assets covering the current liabilities and growing equity, reducing their total debt (note the difference in current maturities of long-term debt) quite a bit compared to Q4’FY25, being helped by their non-core asset sales. This is an overall good financial position, although they don’t have that much cash on hand.

Macro Developments

Something I want to reiterate is the premiumization that should persist in the long-term, which can offer an advantage for companies like STZ that are well-covered from this point of view while their competitors have to fight and build/buy these kinds of brands. As the company mentioned during their Q3 Earnings Report:

The operating environment during the third quarter of fiscal 2026 remained challenged, which was in line with our expectations and relatively consistent with the prior quarter. Demand across the beer, wine, and spirits categories remained soft as consumers continued to exhibit cautious spending behavior resulting from multiple variables, including the cumulative effects of inflation, overall economic uncertainty, and other ongoing socioeconomic factors.

This is important because it has the potential to prevent the company from losing market share the same way normal brands could to the significant risk coming from the retail industry - private brands. This brand power and popularity ultimately represent a moat in this aspect as well, while the common “volume brands” that have nothing really unique are likely at risk of losing over the long-term on top of the premiumization itself.

However, we have to keep in mind that we are currently looking at a weak consumer environment, especially since the pandemic, and beer companies are far from safe from it. Allianz published a survey recently where they highlighted that nearly half of Americans faced more financial pressure at the end of 2025 compared to the beginning of the year, mentioning factors like the costs of day-to-day expenses, low income, too much debt, or even the lack of job security.

Meanwhile, the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index came out a bit better in December, but that’s compared to November, which is the second-lowest level seen in the history of the survey, and we’re still looking at an overall low level, as high prices continue to pressure.

On top of this, there is potential to see higher rates for longer, as rising political/economic uncertainty combined with worries surrounding the Fed’s independence (after Powell’s term ends in May 2026) can affect the market’s perceptions and demand higher rewards (treasury yields) to take the new risks it sees.

Bank of America’s CEO also made a similar point, warning that the market will “punish people” if the Fed’s independence is affected, as it can ultimately hurt businesses and people alike by affecting their cost of debt.

As mentioned before, it’s true that this person still comes with a single vote out of twelve in FOMC rate decisions, and there are others that can prevent them from doing anything they want, but Powell has proven how much volatility this person’s words can cause, at times benign even more influential for the market than the President himself.

Even if the market can just take the new Chair’s statements with a grain of salt, we would still lose a key indicator that used to at least offer a bit of clarity regarding the Fed’s near-term decisions and gave some insights into the economy, which can ultimately cause even more volatility.

Although we’ve seen tariff uncertainty persisting, I believe it’s important to note that even if we see a resolution or agreement to these tariffs, there’s nothing that guarantees that there won’t be another issue or something that brings them back.

The company’s management also commented regarding the impact of tariffs expected in Q4, saying the following regarding their beer business:

During the fourth quarter, while we expect year-over-year volume declines to improve, operating margins are expected to be pressured by fixed cost absorption headwinds, an increase in deprecation expense, and higher tariff expense.

This is why companies should treat these tariffs as a wake-up call and bring production capacity or logistics back to the US, basically hedging against future risks, which is ultimately a key reason why we saw these tariffs in the first place.

However, one of the specific risks for Constellation Brands is that beer provides a wide majority of their revenue, and that side of the business is almost entirely imported from their breweries in Mexico. Unlike competitors such as Molson Coors (TAP) or Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD), which can develop domestic supply chains and brewing capacity, Constellation's rights to their Modelo and Corona brand families are strictly for importation into the US (BUD owns them everywhere else).

This is a massive issue for the company in the current environment, being probably their single biggest risk that’s responsible for a lot of the nearly 50% drop in stock price since 2024. In fact, the company has invested billions into Mexican infrastructure in recent years as part of their supply chain investing strategy, exposing them significantly to political pressure.

As a note, they can't really do much with these brands other than import them in the US because of the license they gained following the 2013 settlement between today’s BUD and Grupo Modelo, limiting them to “Mexican-style” beers - for which they were actually sued (although they won) by BUD before. We’re basically talking about some unique legal “handcuffs” that limit them significantly while also forcing them to depend on their US operations, and the market is reacting to this uncertainty around it.

Risks Reiterated

Overall, although Constellation Brands remains well-positioned for long-term premiumization tailwinds, they are still going to be affected by consumer weakness in the near-term, which can continue for longer due to macro pressure.

Meanwhile, their reliance on Mexico can continue to affect them due to tariffs or heightened political issues, while competition can take advantage of their weakness to steal market share while the company has to focus its efforts on navigating the environment, being legally limited by their agreements when it comes to their biggest brands.

Valuation

Starting with STZ's midpoint $1.35 billion free cash flow expectations in FY26, I'll continue to assume another $1.35 billion in FCF in FY27 due to their elevated CAPEX and tariff pressure, which will then get back to $2 billion from FY28, growing at a 5% CAGR until FY31 (similar to the market's EPS expectations) thanks to their strong financials and future-facing brands that should benefit from premiumization trends in a normal environment, with a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, an 8% discount rate and 174.61 million shares outstanding as of their Q3'FY26 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $38.51 billion, meaning an intrinsic value of $220.56 per share, which is well above the current ~$149.63 per share, which is after the stock jumped following STZ’s Q3 report. Even if we assume a discount rate of 9% for a better margin of safety, we’re still looking at a price of ~$185.47 for a good margin of safety.

Conclusions

STZ remains a Buy, with their recent earnings report proving their ability to navigate this very uncertain and weak consumer environment despite their limitations, with solid free cash flow and financials.

There are risks stemming especially from their dependency on Mexico combined with this environment, but they also managed to prove their resiliency so far (as much as we can ask from a company like this) and still returned significant amounts to shareholders.

data.title: High Current Yield With Solid Credit Rating: A Closer Look At NCZ And Its Preferred Stock NCZ.PR.A
data.description:
We continue our search for financial instruments with minimal credit risk. Today, we will look at Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II (NCZ) and its preferred stock (NCZ.PR.A)

NCZ basic information

  • Total Net Assets: Close to $289.75 million
  • Expenses:

NCZ has a total leverage ratio of close to 34.5%. Its expense ratio is around 4.71%, and the non-leverage expense ratio is around 1.3%. The relative leverage cost is approximately 6.12%.

  • Portfolio

The number of investments in the portfolio of the fund is close to 221, and you can see the top 10 of them below:

NCZ's portfolio is primarily allocated in convertible securities - close to 62.06%. Followed by 32.39% in High Yield Bonds, 3.09% in other, and 2.46% in cash and equivalents.

Below is shown the sector allocation of the fund. The biggest parts are the "Information Technology" sector (23.96%), the "Industrials" sector (15.27%), the "Financials" sector (14.02%), and the "Consumer Discretionary" sector (11.43%).

  • Performance

NCZ is currently priced at $14.18, with around a 10.64% discount. It has a negative 1-year Z-score of 1.87 and a negative 3-year Z-score of 0.25. The premium/discount history of the fund for the past 10 years is as follows:

  • Credit rating

Fitch rates NCZ with an "A" credit rating, making NCZ's preferred stock among the highest-quality.

  • Comparisons:

The following chart shows a total return comparison between NCZ and its peers (from Seeking Alpha). We can see that the fund outperforms most of the time. And below we can see how well it is correlated with the overall market:

And here you can see how well it is correlated with State Street® SPDR® Bloomberg Convertible Securities ETF (CWB):

Preferred stock

Below is shown the preferred stock of NCZ: (NCZ.PR.A)

The stock was issued on 09/04/2018 with a coupon rate of 5.50% and a $1.38 annual payment. The spread to the treasury yield then was 2.51%, and currently it is close to negative 0.68% - we see again that the spread is narrow, also like in our last articles, and this is normal for the sector, as we mentioned more than once. The call date has passed, so it is callable anytime.

The current yield of NCZ.PR.A is above 6.6%. You can see a chart with potential prices and current yields of the stock below:

CEF funds are regulated by the Investment Company Act of 1940 and must maintain a 200% asset coverage ratio. The leverage of NCZ would need its portfolio to lose more than 30% of its value for it to break the leverage limit.

In the chart is shown a comparison between NCZ.PR.A and its peers - CEF preferred stocks with high credit ratings, that trade below par - CY vs % of the PAR. We can see that NCZ.PR.A is at the top of this comparison, but one of the reasons for that might be the fact that its distributions are not qualified.

Conclusion

NCZ and its preferred stock NCZ.PR.A stand out as a good option for investors seeking higher yields with relatively high credit quality. Despite the fund's comparability in the sector, higher leverage and expenses, its "A" credit rating, diversified portfolio, and 1940 Act regulation provide solid protection. Trading below par, with a current yield over 6.6% and non-qualified dividends, this might be the better option for non-US investors or for your IRA.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 01/10/26
data.description:
