data.title: Nova Minerals: Solid Capital Injection And Possible Mineral Production In 2026
data.description:
The race for critical minerals continues, fueled by factors such as heightened federal activity, including direct equity investments in companies like Lithium Americas and MP Materials, as well as policy implementation to support domestic production. Australian company Nova Minerals Limited (NVA), known for its exploration of gold and antimony, gained recognition in October 2025 after receiving a $43.4 million non-dilutive award from the US Department of War for antimony production. The stock is up 195.23% (YoY) and has a market cap of $260 million. In this article, I will explain why Nova Minerals is a hold in view of its liquidity portfolio that will enable it attain its development targets by FY 2026, but is overvalued.

NVA is focused on a solid capital injection in 2026 that I believe will be crucial in ensuring it achieves its production targets, especially in the Estelle Project. Firstly, as of September 2025, NVA explained that it had a total of AUD 86.3 million ($57.75 million) in funding. It included its cash balance of AUD 17.5 million ($11.71 million), the DoW grant of $43.4 million, and a liquid investment of $3.3 million. Additionally, in late December 2025, NVA announced the closure of a public offering of about 2.93 million “American Depositary Shares (ADS) priced at $6.83 per ADS” grossing $20 million. This means that NVA has a total liquidity of about $77.75 million as of 2026.

That said, NVA confirmed in its recent financial report that the Antimony project was fully funded by the DoW’s $43.4 million grant. We must also keep in mind that it is a 24-month award that is supposed to run until 2027, by which time it will have already begun production. It leaves a balance of $34.35 million, which is intended to be used for tier-one gold asset development in FY 2026/27. In FY 2025, the capitalized exploration expenditure (CAPEX) used in the Estelle gold project increased 9% (YoY) to A$100.135 million from A$92.118 million. A 20% (YoY) increase in the CapEx would mean Nova will be required to spend about A$120.16 million ($80.423 million) as CapEx in 2026. The CapEx required would be more than this into 2026 if the company decides to acquire more antimony assets in Pakistan, as has been alleged in various media articles. Keeping in mind that NVA's US domestic antimony production is fully funded for production by 2027. However, the company recently clarified that its “focus was to complete the feasibility study for its gold assets and begin the antimony trisulfide production.” Taking this allegation into consideration, the 100+ tons supposedly to be purchased from Pakistan in Q1 2026 would mean that a ton of antimony would cost about $20,000 (seeing that the first purchase price is expected to be $2 million). Critical to the company is that production of antimony must commence in 2026 as per the DoW funding agreement.

The global antimony market was valued at $2.35 billion in 2025 and is expected to reach $4.85 billion by 2035 (growing at a CAGR of 7.5% within the forecast period). The research also shows that 50% of the antimony produced globally is in Asia, with China controlling the bulk of the production. The demand for antimony has been aggravated by its use in the manufacturing of solar panels, semiconductors, batteries, and ammunition. As it appears, the DoW’s injection appears to be part of the US plan to insulate itself from outside critical mineral sources and boost its ammunition.

It is also vital to note that NVA will seek to first produce the “military-grade antimony trisulfide (Sb₂S₃) for which it received the funding until 2027, before it expands to antimony oxides and the antimony metal itself, which are priced differently. Into 2026, the price of antimony trisulfide (Sb2S3) stands at $51.86 per Kg (in North America) and $22.05 per Kg in Northeast Asia. The 7N High Purity Antimony goes for an average of $385.42 per kg, while the 99.5% Antimony trioxide goes for an average of $17,249 per MT.

For the FY 2026, Nova will need more money as it will also involve seeking permits alongside conducting the feasibility studies of the gold assets. Construction of the Estelle gold mining infrastructure, including resource expansion, is expected to commence in 2028. The US Antimony Corp announced a total contract award of $352 million in late 2025 (from the US Defense Logistics Agency and an Industrial customer) to also produce antimony, and into 2026, they indicated that they are sourcing feedstock from as far as Mexico, as opposed to Alaska.

While the Estelle mining project owned by Nova Minerals does not yet have proven gold reserves (as elucidated in this previous article), it sits in the same geological zone as the Fort Knox mine, owned by Kinross Gold (within the Alaskan area of the Tintina Gold belt). As of the nine months ending on September 30, 2025, Kinross Gold reported that the Fort Knox mine produced 339,299 ounces of gold, up 24.58% (YoY) from 272,357 ounces recorded in the prior year. Sales of $432.8 million realized from Fort Knox (at the time) represented 60.7% of all the revenue Kinross Gold obtained in the US alone, and was the highest in the company’s overall numbers. Since 1996, the company has produced over 7 million ounces of gold (29 years), up 183% from a proven and probable reserves of 230 million tons at 0.3 g/t of gold (or 2.471 million ounces) researched back in 2018. NVA’s resource base stands at a probable 9.9 million ounces in its lifetime, and as seen above, it could increase by 50%-100% or even more (since the feasibility study is ongoing). Keeping in mind that gold production is likely to start in 2028/2029. If the reserves are proven by then, we are likely to see the first annual production range of 300,000 ounces to 500,000 ounces (a midpoint of 400,000 ounces). With the current gold price at about $4,463 per ounce, a production cost of about $2,000 per ounce would mean NVA rakes in an annual sales return of $800 million in the FY 2028/2029. However, this will only be possible if the gold reserves are proven, as at present the company is still in the development stages.

Valuation

I will use the price-to-book ratio because NVA is at the pre-revenue stage (yet to hit commercialization) as it is an exploration and development company. This ratio will evaluate the company’s market price (per share) against its net assets or book value as of 2026.

NVA’s price-to-book (forward) multiple stands at 3.89X. To calculate the market price per share, I will need to identify the forward net assets for FY 2025. Firstly, the net assets for FY 2025 were $109.86 million, indicating an 11.66% (YoY) increase in the net asset value.

As stated earlier, Nova Minerals is expected to start commercialization in 2026, through the production of its first batch of antimony (including possible positive results from gold feasibility studies). NVA's peer, Perpetua Resources, had its net assets increase by almost 600% (YoY) as of September 2025. Perpetua Resources is set to begin construction of its Stibnite Gold -Antimony Project in Idaho in 2026, a move that will increase its asset cover in the year. NVA is also set to commence its production of antimony in 2026 that will also increase its net asset value in the year.

A closer look at NVA shows that since FY 2021, NVA’s net assets have increased by about 108.93% to 2025. By inference, an increase of approximately 100% in its net assets in FY 2026 (with the commencement of production in mind) will likely result in a future net asset value of A$219.71 million ($146.85 million) in FY 2026. To determine the future market share price, I will multiply the forward net asset value ($440.23 million) by the price-to-book ratio (3.89 times), resulting in a future market capitalization of $571.25 million. NVA’s outstanding share count sits at 401.5 million shares. To get the future share price, I will divide the market cap by the outstanding share count, resulting in $1.42 as the future share price. NVA’s stock price stood at $7.36 as of this writing, giving a difference of -418% (YoY). While the analysis shows overvaluation by more than 400%, it is working on a P/B ratio of 3.89X and a 100% (YoY) growth in net assets. Growth beyond this point will increase the stock's value. That is, if net asset value grows by 1000%, then the forward total equity will be $810 million, thereby driving the price towards $7.85.

I will compare the performance of NVA with one of its peers, Perpetua Resources Corp. (PPTA), which is in the gold and antimony business but has yet to make revenues. It is vital to first mention that PPTA operates in the Idaho Area of the Stibnite Gold Project (with revenues expected from 2027/2028). As of September 2025, its NAV stood at $532 million, an increase of 589.2% (YoY) from $77.2 million in the prior year. An increase of about 50% (YoY) would mean PPTA’s total equity value in 2026 will sit at $798 million. We have to keep in mind that NVA is likely to introduce new antimony assets in 2026, while PPTA will probably wait until FY 2027/28.

Forward P/B ratio

Forward NAV (Equity)

Future Market Cap

Future share price

Current price

Valuation
NVA 3.89X $440.23 million $1.712 billion $1.42 $7.36 -418 (YoY) overvalued
PPTA 5.68X $798 million $4.533 billion $37.18 $27.50 +35.21% (YoY) undervalued

The above tabulated analysis shows that PPTA is undervalued by 35.21% (YoY) while NVA is overvalued by 418%. I have confined the analysis to the price-to-book ratio since the company is pre-revenue. The growth in the total equity has also been calculated at a minimum growth rate of 100% (YoY). It is important to consider the possible production of antimony in 2026 and other constructions that will add revenues and assets to the company.

Commencement of military-grade antimony production by NVA in 2026 may increase the company’s chances of commercialization by late 2026/2027 and thereby raise the NAV in 2026.

Risks

This valuation works on the premise that Nova Minerals will commence the production of antimony in 2026, as previously indicated. This production has informed by the decision to increase the NAV by more than 1000% due to the introduction of new assets with higher valuation by the company in 2026. Failure to commence this production will adversely affect the share price as elucidated herein. Further, I also expect positive outcomes from the gold feasibility studies in 2026, which are expected to guide the financing and construction into 2028.

Lastly, the company may dilute its stock to raise capital, especially for conducting the gold feasibility studies, if no other funding sources are secured. In the year ending June 2025, NVA increased its outstanding share balance by 64.82% (YoY) to 401.5 million shares from 243.6 million shares.

So NVA has about 401.5 million in the FY 2025, having closed the FY 2024 with 243.6 million shares. It represents a dilution of about 64%. Still, the company's share momentum in 2025 has been robust, as the stock has an upside of almost 200% (as of this writing). We need to consider the fundamentals that have been discussed in this article, keeping in mind that the company is moving towards a production (of antimony) that is fully funded.

While share dilution remains a major risk for NVA (especially if it runs out of cash), the company has zero debt. Consequently, if it lacks any other funding sources for its gold feasibility project, then it will either go for debt or dilute its stock. I expect more information on this later in March 2026, when the company will likely confirm its commencement of antimony production and progress of the gold feasibility study.

Bottom Line

I have rated Nova Minerals as a hold in light of its robust liquidity portfolio that will likely see it attain its antimony production and gold feasibility studies in 2026. However, the stock is overvalued based on my price-to-book analysis, and I have assumed a possible 100% growth in its total equity or net asset value by 2026. The main risks to this analysis are the failure to commence the antimony production on time, which may hurt the company’s commercialization process and possibly result in share dilution to raise funding in 2026. I await the next earnings date in March 2026 to update my analysis.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JQUA: Balanced Approach With Lighter Allocation To Mega Caps (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The JPMorgan U.S. Quality Factor ETF (JQUA) is a fund that goes beyond a quality-oriented strategy; it also seeks a balanced sector mix and a portfolio construction that gives the fund a relatively conservative profile compared to the equity indexes, such as the Russell 1000. While the sector exposure has not changed much since my last review of the fund in September 2024, its allocation to mega caps has decreased somewhat, exacerbating a mid- and large-cap tilt that may differentiate the fund’s performance from here. The coming sections will break down JQUA’s portfolio across core sectors and compare measures such as valuation, performance, volatility, and tradability with the broader market and other quality ETFs

ETF Description & Highlights

JQUA is an ETF that seeks to enhance returns by investing in U.S. companies with above-average quality and profitability, tracking the JP Morgan US Quality Factor Index.

This index follows a rules-based methodology that selects stocks from the Russell 1000 based on a proprietary quality factor determined through measures including profitability, earnings, and solvency. Stocks are selected across all sectors and weighted according to their market capitalization, while targeting a neutral sector exposure relative to the Russell 1000. The index is also rebalanced on a quarterly basis to reflect current market conditions.

As of December 31, 2025, JQUA had $7.6 billion in assets invested across 209 holdings, with an average market cap of $114.8 billion, which is nearly one-third of the Russell 1000 average market cap of $354.8 billion. With 39.2% of the fund in large caps and 29.4% in mid caps, these two market cap categories make up the majority of the portfolio, while mega caps (18.0%) and small caps (12.3%) correspond to a smaller share of the fund. This highlights a shift away from mega caps compared to my last update on this fund in September 2024, when mega caps accounted for 27.2% of total assets. JQUA’s current market cap profile also marks a contrast with the Russell 1000, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), where mega caps make up 42.2% of the index, due to its market cap-weighted structure.

JQUA’s top ten holdings are NVIDIA (2.05%), Microsoft (2.00%), Berkshire Hathaway (1.98%), Meta Platforms (1.96%), Apple (1.95%), Alphabet (1.95%), Visa (1.91%), Broadcom (1.77%), Johnson & Johnson (1.63%), and Exxon Mobil (1.56%). Together, these positions make up just under 20% of total assets and are concentrated in mega caps from technology and communications services, similar to the broader equity indexes, though these top holdings represent a much higher share of the Russell 1000, for example, where NVIDIA sits near 7%, with Apple and Microsoft each above 5%. The list is complemented by names from financial services, healthcare, and energy, like Visa, Johnson & Johnson, and Exxon Mobil, which give the fund a slightly more balanced distribution than the index.

The following table introduces a list of quality-oriented funds that will serve as the peer group for JQUA in this analysis. The first is the iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF (QUAL), the largest fund in the group by assets under management, tracking the MSCI USA Sector Neutral Quality Index, which uses profitability, earnings stability, and leverage metrics while maintaining sector neutrality. The Invesco S&P 500 Quality ETF (SPHQ) applies quality screens such as ROE, accruals, and financial leverage to the S&P 500 constituents, resulting in a predominantly large-cap profile with market-cap-based weighting. The Dimensional US High Profitability ETF (DUHP) takes a more flexible approach, emphasizing high operating profitability across large and mid caps without formally tracking an index, while the Fidelity Quality Factor ETF (FQAL) tracks a proprietary quality index focused on profitability and earnings consistency. Completing the list, the Vanguard U.S. Quality Factor ETF (VFQY) blends quality metrics with a more pronounced mid- and small-cap footprint, and the Astoria US Equal Weight Quality Kings ETF (ROE) filters 100 stocks based on quality measures and adopts an equal-weight approach.

From a sector allocation perspective, JQUA has 37.2% invested in technology, followed by financial services (13.9%) and consumer discretionary (11.4%), healthcare (9.6%), and industrials (7.5%). Smaller positions go to communication services (6.9%), consumer staples (5.5%), energy (3.2%), and real estate (2.1%), with basic materials (1.5%) and utilities (1.3%) rounding out the portfolio. As a result, relative to the Russell 1000, JQUA is most overweight in technology (+3.6%), offset by underweight exposure to communication services (-3.7%). Despite these gaps, it is fair to say that JQUA has a sector mix that largely mirrors the Russell 1000, which is consistent with its targeted neutral sector positioning relative to the benchmark. Of note, the fund’s sector weights have also largely stayed unchanged since my last article in September 2024, with minor changes such as an increase in technology (+4.1%) primarily due to the sector’s outperformance during this period.

If we break down JQUA’s portfolio across its core sectors, we see that holdings in technology highlight an overweight exposure to the software segment, through positions in Microsoft, Salesforce, Palantir, the data analytics provider, and ServiceNow, the workflow automation platform. Semiconductors are also a meaningful pillar, though they represent a lighter share compared to the Russell 1000. Key holdings are Nvidia, Broadcom, AMD, and Lam Research. Other positions in the sector include Apple, data storage supplier Western Digital, enterprise flash storage provider Pure Storage, and hybrid cloud storage player NetApp.

Holdings in financial services look more balanced with 37 positions across payments, insurance, and market infrastructure, while steering clear of the banking segment. Diversified conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway is the largest sector position, while holdings in consumer finance are also significant through global payment giants Visa and Mastercard. The fund owns insurers such as Chubb, the commercial insurer, Progressive, the auto insurer, Travelers, the P&C carrier, and Arch Capital, the specialty insurer. Holdings in market data and exchanges round out the allocation to financials via names like S&P Global, the ratings and index provider, Intercontinental Exchange, the exchange operator, and Cboe Global Markets, the options exchange player.

JQUA owns 35 companies in consumer discretionary, with a diversified allocation in segments such as travel, dining, and retail. The travel-related exposure is led by global hotel operator Marriott, hotel chain Hilton, online travel agency Expedia, and Booking Holdings. The fund owns restaurant franchisor Yum Brands, McDonald’s, the quick-service leader, and Starbucks, the coffeehouse operator. Holdings in home improvement are concentrated in two household names: Home Depot and Lowe’s, while the specialty retail sleeve includes auto parts retailer O’Reilly Automotive, Ulta Beauty, Best Buy, and pool supplies distributor Pool Corp.

Outside the top three sectors, significant allocations include Alphabet and Meta Platforms in communication services, energy giants Exxon Mobil, Chevron, and ConocoPhillips, consumer staples names such as Procter & Gamble, Colgate-Palmolive, and Philip Morris International, along with retailers Costco and Walmart. Elsewhere, JQUA still owns industrials like Illinois Tool Works, Lincoln Electric, and Waste Management, as well as Linde, the industrial gases supplier in the basic materials sector.

JQUA’s sector mix is arguably highly correlated with most peers. This includes QUAL (0.99), FQAL (0.99), ROE (0.98), and DUHP (0.94), as these funds share a generally neutral allocation compared to broader equity indexes, with over 30% exposure to technology, a low teens position in financial services and consumer discretionary, along with mid to high-single stances in industrials and consumer staples.

Meanwhile, SPHQ and VFQY keep a more conservative allocation profile, with technology below 30%, while maintaining overweight exposure to defensive areas such as consumer staples and healthcare, and also in industrials. Still, sector correlation remains in the 0.70-0.80 range with JQUA, as they keep a fairly large position in core sectors like financial services and healthcare, for instance.

Mid-Range Valuation

JQUA’s 21.0x P/E represents a small 3% discount to the Russell 1000’s 21.7x. The fund’s valuation remains roughly in line with the benchmark, as sector allocation gaps are not so meaningful. In addition, the fund’s stock selection keeps individual sectors’ P/E ratios relatively close to the benchmark as well. The only representative gaps stem from consumer discretionary and healthcare, where holdings trade at 66% and 18% discounts, respectively. This is driven by a few factors, such as no positions in Tesla, Amazon, and Eli Lilly, all trading above 30x P/E, as well as overweight allocations in value plays like Merck (11.9x P/E), Bristol Myers (8.2x), ExxonMobil (17.6x), and Qualcomm (14.3x).

The fund’s growth picture is also mixed. Its historical earnings growth of 9.2% is above the Russell 1000’s 8.2%, driven by earnings growth in financial services and healthcare that has been much higher than the index, driven by overweight positions in names like Idexx and Merck in healthcare and a number of holdings in financial services, including Jack Henry and Fidelity National Financial. The flip side is JQUA’s sales growth of 6.9%, which trails the benchmark’s 7.4%, reflecting widespread slower growth across most sectors, particularly technology, healthcare, and consumer discretionary, and reflecting a portfolio with lighter exposure to high-growth companies such as NVIDIA, Amazon, and Microsoft.

JQUA is close to the Russell 1000 on profitability, but still falls short of the benchmark. Its EBITDA margin of 31.9% lags the Russell 1000’s 36.0% by about 4.1 percentage points, while ROE of 41.2% trails the benchmark’s 43.3%. The technology sector is the weak spot, dragging down the fund’s profitability average, despite relatively stronger numbers in other sectors. This was mostly due to underweight positions in high-margin big techs like Apple, NVIDIA, and Microsoft.

JQUA’s valuation analysis relative to peers can be split into two steps. First, the fund trades at a discount to QUAL (23.6x P/E), SPUQ (22.2x), DUHP (21.4x), and FQAL (22.9x), largely due to these funds’ heavier allocation to large and mega caps at nearly 70%, while JQUA keeps it below 60%, with positions in mid caps (29.4%) and small caps (12.3%) driving valuation down.

On the flip side, VFQY and ROE trade at much lower P/E multiples. This reflects their stronger tilt toward small and mid caps, totaling 69% and 49% of assets, respectively, leading to a valuation profile that resembles a mid-cap fund in the 15x to 17x range. In sum, JQUA’s valuation sits in the middle of the pack, primarily influenced by market cap distribution, as sector mix does not play a key role here, given its somewhat neutral sector exposure.

Performance In Line With Peers' Average

JQUA has performed in line with the Russell in the long term, with a 14.0% annualized return over the last five years. But the fund has lagged more recently. Its 3-year annualized return of 19.1% has not kept pace with the benchmark’s 22.7%, after a strong underperformance in 2024 and 2025, likely driven by its underweight exposure to big techs that led the broader market over this period. Compared to peers, JQUA has delivered returns broadly in line with the group average. However, we can see a clear bifurcation in performance, especially over the 3-year time frame, with QUAL, SPHQ, and FQAL clearly outperforming. Of note, it is fair to say that DUHP and ROE have a limited track record, and more time is needed to better evaluate their performance over a full market cycle.

The fund has seen moderate volatility, with betas in the 0.91-0.92 range, below the Russell 1000 and most peers, though slightly above SPHQ, which is a fund with a more defensive profile, as illustrated by its underweight allocation to technology, for instance. JQUA has also shown a relatively contained price action relative to the broader market, with capture ratios below 1.00. This is particularly evidenced during price declines, as its downside capture ratio in the 95-98 range has been the second lowest, only above SPHQ, which is consistent with the lower betas of these two funds compared to the rest of the peer group.

JQUA's average daily trading volume of $34.4 million is decent, though clearly below funds with heavy volume like QUAL and SPHQ. Still, the fund maintains a tight 0.02% bid-ask spread, which is narrower than most peers. Its 21% turnover is the second lowest in the group, leading to a low 0.12% expense ratio, the lowest among peers. The fund offers a 1.19% dividend yield, slightly higher than peers, though still modest in absolute terms. Its tracking error is slightly higher than the peer group average, while Sharpe ratios of 1.18 (3-year) and 0.73 (5-year) sit near the middle of the peer group, in line with QUAL, but below SPHQ and FQAL over the 3-year window, largely influenced by their strong performance that has played a major role in risk-adjusted returns.

Final Thoughts

Based on an allocation strategy that targets a neutral sector exposure, JQUA maintains a fairly large exposure to sectors such as technology and consumer discretionary, but individual positions in big tech names are much lighter than in broader market indexes, like the Russell 1000. Still, valuations are close to this benchmark, though below some of the mega-cap heavy ETF peers. Meanwhile, with a backdrop that still looks supportive in the sense that inflation appears to be easing and a market that is sitting near new highs, despite potential headwinds like slower growth and trade headlines, my view is that investors are likely to be more selective throughout 2026, which creates a good setup for quality stocks.

With that in mind, JQUA looks well-positioned in this environment, especially if leadership broadens beyond big techs. On the other hand, the fund’s recent underperformance is a reminder of the downside associated with an investment strategy that seems to avoid concentration in any particular stocks, as its top holding, NVIDIA, represents only 2% of total assets. That said, with relatively fair valuation and solid earnings growth, JQUA is a good fit and a long-term hold for risk-aware investors, as illustrated by its below-average volatility, but is probably not the best alternative for those seeking high-momentum strategies. Still, assuming the S&P 500’s earnings growth of 12% for 2026, it is reasonable to expect the fund to deliver a high-single digit to low-teens total return this year, just a bit above the fund’s historical average, considering limited room for P/E multiple expansion, given historically elevated valuations in the overall stock market.

data.title: Duolingo: My Top Pick For 2026
data.description:
Duolingo (DUOL) is one of the few consumer subscription businesses that clears the Rule of 40 while still feeling like a mobile game. It’s got hundreds of millions of users, a ~30% adj. EBITDA margin growing at 40% YoY. Yet, the stock is down ~70% from its May 2025 highs.

In this piece, I’ll walk through why I’ve always wanted to own Duolingo, why I think the market is misreading the recent drawdown, and how I’m thinking about valuation and risk from here.

I initiate coverage on Duolingo with a Strong Buy and a price target of $250 per share.

Duolingo At a Glance

Consumer edtech app focused on language learning, math, music, chess, literacy, and English proficiency testing with a goal to make high-quality education free and accessible to everyone. Key KPIs as of 3Q25:

  • Monthly Active Users (MAUs): 135M+ (>20% growth YoY)
  • Daily Active Users (DAUs): 50M+ (>30% growth YoY)
  • Paid subscribers: 11M+ (>40% growth YoY)
  • ~75% of revenue from subscription, ~10% ads, ~5% in-app purchases, rest is DET or misc.
  • ~30% adj. EBITDA margin

Duolingo Roots and Where We Are Today

From 2009 to 2011, Duolingo was an idea at Carnegie Mellon. Luis von Ahn (Guatemalan-born CMU professor, created reCAPTCHA) and student Severin Hacker came up with the idea (and founded it) because learning English in Guatemala was expensive (more here).

In 2012, Duolingo launched with a huge waitlist. In 2013, it was the #1 education app on Android with ~5M users. It received funding from USV, NEA, Kleiner Perkins, and Google. By 2015, Duolingo had 100M registered users. The company initially made money through a crowdsourced translation model.

In 2016, Duolingo launched DET (Duolingo English Test), an online English exam accepted by universities and introduced Duolingo Plus subscription (ad-free, offline, extra hearts), getting rid of crowdsourcing model. By 2019, Duolingo was valued at $1.5B in the private markets with ~30M active users

DUOL IPO’d in July 2021 at $103/share and the stock popped >30% on day one. The company had strong growth through and after COVID. Recently, the company is aiming to shift from “language app” to “general learning app” with their strong AI layer and huge user base: they launched Duolingo ABC (for kids), Math, Music, Chess, and Duolingo Max (AI-powered tier).

All You Need to Know About Duolingo’s Products

Duolingo Language App

40+ languages; courses structured into bite-sized lessons. Heavy gamification with streaks, XP, leagues, friends, hearts, characters, and quests. Targets beginners = lower intermediate; advanced coverage is improving via new path + AI.

Pricing:

  • Free tier with ads and limited features
  • Super Duolingo: $12.99/mo or $84/year for ad-free unlimited
  • Duolingo Max: $30/mo or $169/year for “Super” + AI-powered features
  • Family Plan (up to 6 users): $120/year for “Super” or $240/year for “Max”

These prices tend to change often, Duolingo has a lot of discounts available at times free trials from 2-days to 14-days. The price in-app for iOS or Android is 30% higher vs the website. So, these numbers vary heavily, and the average revenue per subscriber is unknown at this time.

Duolingo English Test (DET)

Online, at-home, AI-scored proficiency exam. It’s accepted by thousands of universities globally (including the top US schools). The price point is $59-$70 for a single test, much lower than TOEFL/IELTS ($250-300 per test) with frictionless distribution.

Math, Music, and Chess

  • Math is basic arithmetic, early-school content, mostly for kids and refreshers.
  • Music is for learning how to read notes, piano basics, rhythms, and ear training.
  • Chess will teach you how to play chess, but the best part is the tactics and PvP inside the app.

All of these products are bundled with language subscriptions.

Investment Thesis

To be transparent, I’ve wanted to get into Duolingo for a while now, but the price, to me, has shown a less attractive risk/reward output, until today. In my thesis, I will explain why I want to be invested in the business, why now is better than ever, and why current prices are cheap.

Part #1: Why Invest in Duolingo

1.0 What business is Duolingo really in?

I think most people think: “language-learning app”. I see Duolingo as a consumer learning platform that behaves like a social app, monetizes like SaaS, and increasingly teaches like a human tutor or better.

Duolingo has built addictive daily learning behavior at global scale, then layered subscription economics on top of it, and now AI lets them upgrade the “teacher” inside the app. Everything else ladders into that. Cool read to learn more here.

2.0 Why the product works so well

Duolingo is engaging, but it’s also so efficient at creating learning outcomes. It works so well. Duolingo gives structure and progression, reduces friction so anyone can start, reinforces behavior through streaks & social pressure, AND measurably teaches useful language skills.

This is very important because it very much justifies subscriptions (which could be priced much higher, and likely will be in the future), gives credibility for the English Test, and raises the bar vs competitors and other AI tools.

The moat is habit + curriculum + outcomes, at internet scale. Once you have that, monetization is inevitable.

3.0 The real “efficiency” flywheel

Think of Duolingo as having two compounding loops.

Loop 1: Product/Learning loop. Data is everything in this product. Think about it, the more users you have and the more they engage, the more you learn about them over time. Not just learn about an individual, but you learn about all sorts of individuals across all sorts of languages and levels, and you continuously better understand how to perfectly teach a user. The more data Duolingo has, the better its personalization and outcomes are. The better those are, the more word-of-mouth and the more users it brings in. And the loop keeps going.

Loop 2: Business/Monetization. This one is like any other tech business. You have more users, some pay for a subscription, that makes money that Duolingo can reinvest in content + AI, the product improves, and conversion improves accordingly.

Both loops reinforce each other, and neither requires paid marketing. Duolingo is growing 20-40% YoY in terms of users-subscribers. This flywheel is what compounds that growth.

4.0 AI

Some may see AI as a threat. AI is the way Duolingo feels closest to a human tutor (this is particularly for Max features like Roleplay & Explain-My-Answer).

AI adds emotional realism, fixes speaking practice gaps, and justifies that higher-priced tier. In other words, a way to scale content cheaply.

AI leads to faster course creation, more languages, and depth in existing languages, which then leads to a better experience and, thus, stronger retention.

Going forward, Duolingo is just a platform. AI is and will continue to be their focus and their engine.

5.0 English Test is an underrated asset

The English Test, in my view, is proof that Duolingo isn’t just an app or entertainment that people love, it’s credible! Right? Universities are accepting it, students need it, it strengthens the brand, it builds international trust, and monetizes learning outcomes.

And economically, DET is high-margin, a recurring need, and trust-based… aka exactly what we as investors like. Additionally, there’s a ton more white space. As shown in figure above, IELTS and TOEFL have 2.5x the # of programs as Duolingo (market share for them to grab). On top of that, this is just an English test – what about Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, German, etc.? Time will tell.

6.0 Thoughts on competitive risk

In late 2023, Google introduced a new feature in search, enabling users in select markets to translate to/from English to practice speaking, with expansion to other countries/languages. Users leverage 3-5 min practice sessions, receive personalized feedback, etc. and Google is partnering with teachers, ESL/EFL, and others in this.

Then there’s the thought of translation devices. I can go to Asia with my phone or a dedicated device such as Meta glasses or whatever to go about my life with those devices.

While Google & other large companies with tons of resources, strong balance sheets have the ability to increase competition, Duolingo will always be the default place. Real-time translation has been demoed at Google I/O for ~10 years. Yet, demand for language learning has only gone up. At the end of the day, there are 2 main user buckets: (1) hobby/culture learners: similar to chess, computers being better doesn’t kill the hobby; and (2) people learning English for opportunity: they want to actually know the language, not just live behind a translation device.

Additionally, Duolingo owns mindshare, it has a 10-year data advantage, it has habit loops, structured pedagogy, institutional credibility, and a playful consumer brand. Language learning is also emotionally vulnerable (people don’t want a tool or a teacher; they want a coach that doesn't judge them or see them fail).

7.0 What “multi-subject expansion” truly is

The way I think about it is: some skills are universal (reading, math, music, chess, English). They can all be taught through progressive drills and spaced repetition. Duolingo already cracked habit formation and content delivery.

So, each new subject: increases the reasons to open the app, increases family adoption, makes the subscription stickier, raises lifetime value, and supports bundles or tiering. Even if traffic from math/music is small now, they increase gravity around the core platform.

Part #2: Why Now

1.0 The #1 investor debate today

The most important question investors and analysts want to know is “How fast can DAUs keep growing and for how long?”

DAU growth is slowing from crazy high levels but is still strong and stable in absolute terms (28% YoY in 3Q25 vs 39% in 2Q25). Downloads are still growing high-single to low-double digits.

However, a big misconception is that “growth” is mostly people coming back, not just new installs. DAU is daily active users at the end of the day, not related to new users at all.

According to a JPM survey (Internet/Online Education Fall 2025 Survey (1,038 total responses)), only 28% of respondents are new to Duolingo in the last month. ~39% have been using it for more than a month, and ~30% are resurrected (recently returned after a pause).

For context, you will only get access to this survey if you have access to Bloomberg Terminal or JPM Equity Research. I cannot link it and cannot share it publicly, but in this report I will share my key takeaways.

On behavior, 86% of daily language learners have at least a 7-day streak; 40% sit in the 7-30 day band, ~12% are at 365+ days.

So, right now, Duolingo is more of a habit machine than crazy downloads. Notifications, streaks, and Energy are the real retention engine.

Additionally, I very much think there’s a seasonality aspect to this and that 3Q25 is the worst of it. This is obviously skeptical but I think it is reasonable to think that traffic would naturally spike during New Year’s resolutions or back-to-school season. Adding on, Duolingo runs promotions around year-end/New Year in late December through January (you can see them on the app now) and back-to-school (August/September). The company typically experiences an uptick in paid subs & monetization around year-end. Trends then moderate deeper into 1Q and 2Q before ramping around back-to-school. Overall, bookings & revenue should peak in 4Q.

Nonetheless, there are 3 main things I like about the investor focus today. In 3Q25, investors got hit with uncertainty due to a tiny bit lower growth than expected. Firstly, the growth isn’t new users, it's primarily (50-70%+) users coming back, which I believe is heavily misunderstood. Secondly, the fact that investors are now focused on DAU instead of subscriptions or revenue or if the business is real, is a positive in my view, sentiment is better. Thirdly, Seasonality may be a huge momentum boost for growth, particularly for 4Q25 and 1Q26.

2.0 Shift to Energy System

For those that need a refresher, Duolingo used to use Hearts up until recently this year. You mess up, you lose a heart = you are locked out (felt kind of like punishment). Now, Duolingo is using Energy, more like “lives + stamina”, which is much better as it rewards accuracy, limits spammy grinding, makes each session more intentional, and gives a cleaner upgrade path into paid.

According to the same JPM survey cited earlier for Fall 2025, ~50% of users still only have Hearts, 34% know about Energy but don’t have it yet, and 16% actually have the Energy system now. Among users with Energy, 39% say they increased usage vs 28% who say they decreased usage (rest is unchanged).

Throughout 4Q25 and 2026, according to data and thinking conceptually, I'm confident Energy will drive higher DAUs, subscriptions, time spent in app, and conversion as they continue to roll it across iOS and Android. How much? Great question. I don’t have an answer, the market doesn’t either. However, I lean very positive in this change and think there’s a ton of room to exceed expectations, especially given the fact that new users are automatically starting with Energy.

3.0 The best type of marketing

The majority of Duolingo users started because of TikTok/YouTube/Instagram. Friends/family referrals are now the top driver.

In fact, 46% say they came from a friend/family recommendation. Unhinged marketing has been toned down, while international social virality is still very strong. So, the growth model has shifted from virality spike to word-of-mouth + resurrected users, with social still important but less U.S. heavy.

4.0 Duolingo Max is now a real second tier

Max is now framed as the “AI tutor version” of Duolingo. It includes video calls with human-like interaction, “Explain my answer,” and personalized difficulty/feedback/richer practice features, it’s unbelievable work.

For some more JPM survey data, 43% of users are aware Max exists, 7% of Duolingo users report subscribing to Max, and 36% either intend to subscribe or already are. ~80% of Video Call users are satisfied, and ~34% say video calls made them use Duolingo more.

I’d think Max is ~8% of paid subscriptions today, going to ~10% by year-end, making up ~19% of subscription bookings. In 2026, as AI continues to strengthen, who knows what that number will be as Max continues to take share from Super.

Overall, Max is starting to look like a real ARPU driver, with clear differentiators vs Super or even generic AI tools / human tutors, and a way to monetize the most serious learners without touching the casual base.

5.0 Chess is no longer a side quest

Chess reached 1M+ DAUs, faster growth than any other subject. Only ~3% of users currently learn Chess, but ~26% say they intend to. Awareness however, is still low (~14%). PvP mode is coming to iOS first, Android after.

Same pattern shows up in Math and Music interest curves (meaningful intent, low current usage, still early). This to me, like DET, continues to back the idea that Duolingo is a learning / brain-training platform (not “languages”). Different subjects lead to more reasons for daily engagement, and more subjects lead to more reasons not to churn, all of which leads to higher DAU/MAU (which is again, the current investor focus).

6.0 What drives the moat

Motivation is very “game design,” not academic or what we are used to. As per the survey, this is why people open the app:

So, the repeat behavior is anchored in notifications = streak anxiety = identity (Duo, steak, score). That’s the moat against static textbook apps, generic AI chatbots that don’t create a daily ritual and more formal but less sticky tools.

7.0 Competition Continues to Fall

Among the 1k+ survey respondents from JPM’s survey, 62% use Duolingo, 26% Rosetta, 20% Babbel, and 20% Speak. Speak is the only one that rose in share, the one to watch as it’s most focused on spoken / conversational practice. I think Speak is mostly taking share from traditional platforms, not DUOL. Either way, this is a new focus for DUOL, and as mentioned previously with their flywheel, I think DUOL has the resources, tools, and data to blow any competition out of the water. The key takeaway and the more attractive part of this, is that in 1.5 years, market share has grown from 47% to 62%, proving their dominance.

7.1 AI Tools

As shown above, ~41% say they’ve seen inconsistent results from AI language tools, 23% say AI tools are highly accurate, 25% use both AI + regular learning tools, 23% might replace tools with AI, 27% say they will not start using AI tools at all, and 5% currently “only use AI” (not shown in figure above). Make of this data whatever you like, but I believe this shows that users still want structure, trusted brand, visible progress. AI is seen as helpful but unreliable.

In the end, I also think that the hard part of language learning is not access to explanations. The hard part is staying engaged daily for a long time and having a structured curriculum. Duolingo builds a habit, gamifies it with a perfectly tailored curriculum. LLMs do not do that.

Since AI risk and competition is genuinely seen as such a big risk and notable discount to Duolingo’s valuation, I think 2026 will continue to prove why it shouldn’t be as big of a risk or discount.

8.0 Monetization pacing + pricing power

Monetization is a central part of the thesis to invest in DUOL, but management is pacing it well in my view. Only ~9% of LTM MAUs are paid subscribers, a ton of headroom for more. Most paid users are on annual or family plans, which are stickier and better LTV. Majority of language learners spend $9.99/mo or less. Only ~29% spend $15.99 or more on language tools.

Management has explained they don’t want to over-monetize and damage the habit/product feel (this was heavily reiterated in 3Q25 earnings transcript by Luis/CEO). The message to investors is literally “we could push harder now, but we’re playing for LTV and runway”.

As a long-term investor, there’s a ton of room for upside here in the long-term. As a short-term investor, the pricing power is uncertain as to how much and when benefits will be reaped. I’m a long-term investor, and as you can imagine, optimistic on Duolingo. As such, the fact that pricing power is an uncertainty today, to me, is a catalyst for when we get more clarity.

9.0 Globalization

I showed the table earlier, but once again on DUOL DAU growth rates here:

UCAN (US/Canada) is growing 8%, LATAM low-30s%, EMEA ~30% and APAC low-50s%. So, the fastest is not the US anymore; it’s APAC and LATAM. Cross-referencing data on what languages users are currently learning:

English is only 29% as of October (vs 24% in March for context), while Spanish has been at ~55% over the past year. This lines up with the idea that English learners outside the US drive a lot of future monetization. The brand is increasingly global, not American-centric.

The market, in terms of DAU, is no longer about America in my view, its about LATAM, APAC, EMEA learning English. People outside the US/Canada want to learn English for opportunity! In the US, learning languages is seen more as a hobby, unless you have more of an international job. This is the way I see it, as a dual citizen of the US and Spain (with most of my family living in Spain, some in Brazil, and the only family member living in the US) . I don’t have data for motives of learning language per language, but I like to think there’s a lot more opportunity than expected across seas.

Part #3: Current Prices are Cheap

This part is the most important part of why I am investing in Duolingo today. Everything else I have said is part of my thesis on why I want to be invested in the company and why this year and 2026 is exciting. But at the end of the day, everything is about price. If you think about it, you will buy anything at the right price (i.e., $0.01).

1.0 What happened, why is Duolingo down ~50% YTD?

Duolingo reported 3Q25 earnings and the stock dropped 30-50% shortly after. Management guided future bookings growth a bit lower than Wall Street expected (22% vs 24%!)… Bookings is seen as an early signal of future revenue because people prepay annual subscriptions. That single line item spooked the market and the stock tanked, that’s the sole reason.

Nothing “broke” in the business, the fundamentals didn’t change, the stock rerated because forward growth expectations slightly changed in one metric. The important business metrics were still strong:

  • DAUs still growing 30-35% YoY
  • MAUs up to 135M
  • Paid subs up to 11.5M+ (growing 40%)
  • FCF and earnings still growing fast

As a long-term investor, this is all that matters.

2.0 What people and headlines say caused it

The market narrative put simply: “growth is slowing faster than we thought. If bookings aren’t accelerating, the multiple shouldn’t be this high”

And there are 3 main anxiety loops:

  1. “Bookings miss = future slowdown = stock is too expensive”: Investors equate slower bookings growth to weaker demand, lower lifetime value, lower valuation multiple in other words. So the reaction was: cut the multiple hard.
  2. “AI and real-time translation will reduce the need to learn languages”: This created a second fear where if AI makes language learning unnecessary, Duolingo’s long-term TAM could shrink. So some connect slower bookings with an AI risk and say that maybe it’s already showing up.
  3. “Engagement spike was temporary”: one can also argue that virality, Duo memes, “revive Duo” campaign artificially juiced growth and now we’re seeing the “normalization.”

I don’t think this is a “dumb” view, I think it’s incomplete.

3.0 What actually happened

I think the stock fell not because the business is deteriorating, but because management made a deliberate strategic decision. Luis (CEO), in the 3Q25 earnings call basically said: “We’re at a unique moment in education because of AI. We can build an app that teaches as well as a human tutor and is more engaging. That could mean billions of users, not hundreds of millions.”

Priority of Luis is users and teaching quality > short-term monetization knobs. Meaning they could push bookings higher if they wanted to, but they’re intentionally refusing to do it.

For example: Free users get 25 energy units per day. If Luis cuts it to 20, bookings go up because more people run out and pay. But if DAUs go down, people get annoyed and churn. Under the new philosophy, Luis refuses to launch that, even if it boosts near-term bookings or revenue.

As I’ve mentioned before, what investors care about is DAUs. This is what I’m focused on, and what I believe long-term investors should be focused on. However, the market is moody and hates uncertainty. This bookings situation is exactly that, not a change in fundamentals. Misunderstood.

I see this as founder-led, product-first, or exactly the type of thing that happened with Netflix back in 2022 when the stock dropped 75% due to a slight drop in users in one quarter (and since then has risen 6x+).

3.1 The “AI kills Duolingo” idea

AI translation already existed before ChatGPT and other tools. Duolingo still doubled revenue. People learn languages for identity, connection, jobs, pride, cognitive challenge. Just like Chess, computers destroy humans, but millions still want to learn the game. AI may change the tools, but will never change the desire to learn.

3.2 And Importantly…

I’m still seeing all the core KPIs trending up. MAUs, DAUs, Subscriptions, Revenue, FCF, EPS are all exceeding expectations and being revised higher.

The real story is that the business trajectory is the same (and I think the market agrees), but the narrative around valuation has changed. Valuation narratives change the fastest.

4.0 Current prices are a Christmas miracle

I am not bullish because the stock dropped, I’m bullish because:

4.1 The business looks better than ever

  • Durable engagement
  • Recurring subscriptions
  • Huge TAM
  • Max scaling
  • New verticals (math, music, chess)
  • AI improving product quality

In Bezos terms, in the short term, the market is a voting machine. In the long term, the market is a weighing machine. A founder’s job is to build a “heavy” company, not babysit the stock. In his philosophy, Duolingo is getting heavier.

4.2 The market gave us a cheaper entry into the same compounding machine

Before the drop, I already believed in 20%+ FCF growth was reasonable for several years, recurring revenue reduces downside risk, and founder culture increases long-term odds.

Now, same thesis, lower price, more margin of safety. If I liked it at higher prices, and the core thesis is all the same, then mathematically, I like it a whole lot more now.

4.3 The mindset of management at this moment

I specifically respect that management has chosen user experience over squeezing revenue; they were honest about it on the call, and they’re investing heavily into AI + efficacy rather than optics. I prefer CEOs who think in decades.

Overall, the stock crashed because Wall Street repriced future growth expectations (not because fundamentals changed), and I think that’s exactly when long-term investors should lean in (and definitely not tap out).

Duolingo Valuation

How do you even value Duolingo? There’s 2 issues. First, there’s really no good comparable companies. Secondly, it’s a fast growing tech company (generally means intrinsic valuations are more inaccurate). Regarding the first concern, I think the answer to that is building a comprehensive comps universe. For the second concern, while I agree, Duolingo is also a profitable business with recurring/predictable revenues (using the exit multiple method, a DCF is still useful).

Comps Universe

I will use 4 comps buckets:

  1. Consumer subscription (Netflix, Spotify, SiriusXM, Match, Peloton, Bumble, Chegg, Nerdy) because of DUOL’s primary monetization and recurring consumer feature.
  2. Interactive media & gaming (EA, Take-Two, Roblox, Unity, AppLovin, Ubisoft) since DUOL behaves like a gamified engagement platform with daily use and retention is driven higher by product design.
  3. Education (Pearson, Udemy, Coursera, Chegg) due to the same end market (learning), similar demand drivers, but slower growth (adds sector floor valuation or being conservative).
  4. Product-led Rule of 40 subscription platforms (Cloudflare, Datadog, ServiceNow, CrowdStrike, Snowflake) because DUOL clears the rule of 40 (revenue growth of 41% YoY in 3Q25 + adj. EBITDA margin of 29% is 70%!) and the business models are similar (adds more of a bullish multiple).

I show other metrics on there such as growth, beta, financials, and a range of multiples. Ultimately, these companies tend to trade on forward multiples and most on EBITDA. Based on this comps and my view I believe Duolingo deserves to trade at 30x NTM EBITDA, which equates to a price tag of $240 per share and a premium of ~33% to the current price of $180 as of writing.

DCF Analysis

Using consensus estimates from S&P Capital IQ, a discount rate representative of the cost of equity with an assumed expected market return of 10% (generally 8-10%), current risk-free-rate of 4.13%, current beta of 1.7, and a conservative exit EBITDA multiple of 25x, I arrived at an implied share price of $257 (43% premium) and a 14.1% discount rate.

Taking the comps and DCF valuations into account, what this means to me is twofold. First, I think Duolingo is undervalued at any price under $250 per share and fairly valued from $240 to $300 per share. I think $270+, with today’s estimates/financials and market rates, is where I think the risk/return of the stock may be less favorable to invest (same price point of DUOL before 3Q25 was announced, when I resisted investing).

Irrelevant, but funny to think of $200 vs $300 price per share given DUOL was trading at $540 per share back in May of 2025!

Risks

I’ve addressed a lot of the concerns of Duo in this write up. So, the risks below are more representative of risks to my assumptions/optimism:

  1. Any sign of growth deceleration will put pressure on the multiple. If DAU growth slows, we are in big trouble, and basically everything about my thesis is wrong. If anything else slows but DAU continues to accelerate, I’m not too worried.
  2. Competition. As mentioned, I’m not worried about competition. However, this is a market with relatively low switching costs.
  3. If pace of optimization slows (i.e., enhancements don’t resonate with consumers), users & paid subscribers growth could slow more than expected (especially since 90% of user growth is organic).
  4. Volatility. DUOL shares tend to trade at high multiples, relatively. There’s no “traditional” valuation support. There is no perfect comps, no accurate intrinsic valuation analysis. Valuation is very much based on the current mood/sentiment of the market, and as such, DUOL will be very volatile over time.

Concluding Thoughts

Stepping back, long-term investing comes down to understanding a business first and the stock second. Duolingo has a very large and diverse user base, strong engagement, improving monetization, and a product that continues to evolve. There will always be debate around valuation, competitive pressure, AI risk, and the durability of growth (debates I think are healthy). But when I look at the fundamentals today, the business is executing extremely well and much better than anticipated (despite the 2% deceleration in bookings guidance).

I may be wrong on timing, and there will almost certainly be volatility along the way. But when a company with durable engagement and recurring revenue experiences a ~70% reset in price without any deterioration in the underlying business, I am more than comfortable in my long-term view. In the end, this is a Rule of 70 (41% revenue growth YoY in 3Q25 and 29% adj. EBITDA margin) industry leader trading at only ~20x NTM EBITDA today.

For now, I see Duolingo as a heavily misunderstood, founder-led compounder that has been repriced more on short-term nerves than on fundamentals. As such, that’s the perfect time to enter a business, and I’m excited to initiate coverage on DUOL with a Strong Buy and $250 price target.

data.title: China Avoids Deflation In 2025 As December CPI Hits 34-Month High
data.description:
By Lynn Song, Chief Economist, Greater China

China's consumer price index inflation rose 0.8% year-on-year in December to a 34-month high, bringing the full-year CPI to 0.0%. This indicates that China avoided deflation in 2025. Inflation has shown signs of bottoming out and should pick up further in 2026.

Inflation continues to trend higher

China's consumer price inflation rose 0.8% year-on-year in December, up from 0.7%, the highest level since February 2023. The read came in line with market expectations.

As in November, the acceleration in inflation remains primarily attributable to rising food prices. Food inflation picked up to a 1.1% pace YoY, a 14-month high. Fresh vegetable and fresh fruit prices saw the largest month-on-month upticks - up 18.2% and 4.4% YoY, respectively.

Pork prices continued to fall, but saw a slightly smaller drag in year-on-year terms, rising 0.4pp to -14.6% YoY. The YoY level has risen for three consecutive months. We expect the drag from pork price deflation to turn around this year.

Non-food inflation, on the other hand, remained unchanged at 0.8% YoY. Household appliance prices rose 5.9% YoY as we began to see an impact from 2024's trade-in policy discounts feed through the economy. Services inflation, in the form of tourism (2.1%) and healthcare (2.9%), generally outpaced goods inflation.

The transportation and communication category continued to weigh on inflation, down 2.6% YoY, primarily due to declines in transportation (-1.9%), fuel (-8.2%), and communication appliances (-0.7%).

We’re also seeing the continued impact of the property market malaise, with the residence component of inflation in deflation at -0.2% YoY, driven by a -0.3% YoY drop in rents. Core inflation was unchanged for a third consecutive month at 1.2% YoY.

Producer price index inflation rose to -1.9% YoY, up from -2.2% in November. This level marked a 16-month high and the 39th consecutive month of PPI deflation.

Food prices continued to push inflation higher in December

Is the worst over for China's deflationary pressure?

After three straight years of deflationary pressures, could we finally see inflation pick up a little more convincingly in 2026?

Various factors suggest that this could be possible:

  • Non-food inflation shows a mixed picture but has generally been benign in 2H25.
  • Food prices were in deflation for 9 of 12 months in 2025, and the base effect will likely lead to a pickup in YoY levels in 2026. A turnaround of the pork cycle would further contribute to upward food prices.
  • China's anti-involution push could help alleviate downward price pressures, though it remains unclear how much of an impact we will see.
  • Potential new avenues for supporting consumption on top of trade-in policies, which, thanks to discounts, tend to exacerbate deflationary pressure.

As such, we're looking for 0.9% CPI inflation in 2026. While this remains well short of the 2% inflation target pursued by many central banks globally, it would nonetheless be a healthy development after three consecutive years of annual CPI inflation of 0.2% or lower.

Despite expectations of a recovery, inflation remains relatively low and should not preclude further monetary easing this year. We continue to expect there's a case for further rate cuts this year, with a 10 bp cut sometime in 1H26.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
